content.body: Two other proposals (protocols and structs) currently provide for a new way to define methods. We could simplify both of these considerably I think by just dropping the methods from them and deferring to call-this.
Regarding the conflict between call-this and extensions, I believe this can be resolved by removing one of the syntactic options from call this:

* Regarding the conflict between call-this and extensions, I believe this can be resolved by removing one of the syntactic options from call this: `o..ns.m()`. If we instead require this to be `o..(ns.m)()`, then there is no conflict.
content.formatted_body: * Regarding the conflict between call-this and extensions, I believe this can be resolved by removing one of the syntactic options from call this: o..ns.m(). If we instead require this to be o..(ns.m)(), then there is no conflict.

m.new_content.body: Regarding the conflict between call-this and extensions, I believe this can be resolved by removing one of the syntactic options from call this: `o..ns.m()`. If we instead require this to be `o..(ns.m)()`, then there is no conflict.
m.new_content.formatted_body: Regarding the conflict between call-this and extensions, I believe this can be resolved by removing one of the syntactic options from call this: o..ns.m(). If we instead require this to be o..(ns.m)(), then there is no conflict.

content.body: I mean I was wondering if we could even get away with only allowing `o..m()` and not having a place for arbitrary expressions there at all.
content.formatted_body: I mean I was wondering if we could even get away with only allowing o..m() and not having a place for arbitrary expressions there at all.

content.body: Then there's also the apparently steady stream of proposals for adding methods to built-in types. Having "extension methods" (via call-this) provides a compelling way to try things out without standard monkey-patching risks.
